docs.title: DebugBox
docs.text:
Multi-variant Docker debugging toolkit for Kubernetes and cloud-native environments

Choose exactly the right size for your debugging task — no bloat, no waiting.

Variant Size Best For Image lite ~14 MB Network/DNS checks ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:lite balanced (default) ~46 MB Daily Kubernetes troubleshooting ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox power ~104 MB Packet capture & deep forensics ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:power

Also available on Docker Hub: mibtisam/debugbox:*

Supports amd64 and arm64 (Apple Silicon, Graviton, Raspberry Pi) out of the box.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
Debug a running pod in seconds:

bash kubectl debug my-pod -it --image=ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox

Or launch a standalone session:

bash kubectl run debug --rm -it --image=ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox --restart=Never

Now you're inside with curl, dig, tcpdump, vim, strace, and more.

→ Full quick start guide | Installation

docs.title: Why DebugBox?
docs.text:
Modern pods often lack basic debugging tools. DebugBox gives you three focused variants so you only pull what you need.

14 MB for basic connectivity · 46 MB for daily debugging · 104 MB only when you need advanced forensics

Faster pulls. Faster debugging.

→ See the full motivation

docs.title: Local Development Setup
docs.text: Build, test, and contribute to DebugBox locally.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Docker (or Podman with alias docker=podman)
  • Make (GNU make)
  • Git
  • Python 3.8+ (for MkDocs documentation preview)

docs.title: Build All Variants (Multi-Architecture)
docs.text:
Builds all three variants for both amd64 and arm64:

bash make build-all

Output: debugbox:lite-local debugbox:balanced-local debugbox:power-local

docs.title: Build Single Variant
docs.text: bash make build-lite # ~14 MB make build-balanced # ~46 MB make build-power # ~104 MB

docs.title: Build for Specific Architecture
docs.text: bash PLATFORM=linux/arm64 make build-balanced PLATFORM=linux/amd64 make build-power

docs.title: Lint Dockerfiles
docs.text:
Runs hadolint to check Dockerfile syntax and best practices:

bash make lint

Uses local hadolint if available, otherwise runs containerized version.

docs.title: Security Scanning
docs.text:
Scans images with Trivy for vulnerabilities (HIGH/CRITICAL only):

bash make scan

Requires build-all first (auto-triggered).

docs.title: Run All Tests
docs.text:
Executes smoke tests on all three variants:

bash make test-all

Auto-builds and lints first.

docs.title: Test Single Variant
docs.text:
bash make test-lite make test-balanced make test-power

What's tested: Each variant runs tests/smoke.sh to verify: - Essential tools are present - Shells work correctly - Network tools function - Helpers are available (balanced+)

docs.title: Start Local Server
docs.text:
bash mkdocs serve

Opens at http://localhost:8000 with live reload.

docs.title: Make a Change
docs.text:
1. Edit Dockerfile: bash vim dockerfiles/Dockerfile.balanced

  2. Edit docs: bash vim docs/manifest.yaml

  3. Build locally: bash make build-balanced

  4. Test your changes: bash make test-balanced

  5. Preview docs: bash mkdocs serve

docs.title: Quality Gates
docs.text:
Before committing, run the full pipeline:

bash make lint # ✅ Dockerfile syntax make build-all # ✅ All variants build make test-all # ✅ All tests pass make scan # ✅ No vulnerabilities

Or shortcut:

bash make check # Runs lint → build-all → test-all → scan

docs.title: Make Targets Reference
docs.text: Command Purpose make help Show all available commands make build-all Build all variants (amd64 + arm64) make build-<variant> Build single variant (lite/balanced/power) make test-all Test all variants make test-<variant> Test single variant make lint Lint all Dockerfiles make scan Security scan with Trivy make check Full pipeline (lint → build → test → scan) make clean Remove local images

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
Variable Default Description LOCAL_TAG local Local build tag suffix PLATFORM linux/amd64,linux/arm64 Target architectures NO_CACHE false Disable Docker build cache

Example: bash PLATFORM=linux/amd64 NO_CACHE=true make build-all

docs.title: Build fails with exec /bin/sh: exec format error
docs.text:
This happens when building for multiple architectures on a system without binfmt/QEMU support.

Choose one of the following:

Option 1: Enable multi-architecture builds (recommended)

Use this if you want to build for linux/amd64 and linux/arm64:

bash docker run --privileged --rm tonistiigi/binfmt --install all

Option 2: Build for a single architecture

Use this if you don’t want multi-arch builds.

First, check your system architecture:

bash uname -m

Then build accordingly:

```bash

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
DebugBox is a container image — no installation required.

Simply pull from either registry:

```bash

docs.title: Docker Hub (mirror)
docs.text:
mibtisam/debugbox mibtisam/debugbox:lite mibtisam/debugbox:power ```

All images support amd64 (Intel/AMD) and arm64 (Apple Silicon, Graviton, Raspberry Pi).

Note: Unqualified names (debugbox) always refer to the balanced variant.

docs.title: Tag Formats
docs.text:
For production, always pin versions:

bash ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:1.0.0 # Balanced v1.0.0 ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:lite-1.0.0 # Lite v1.0.0 ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:power-1.0.0 # Power v1.0.0

→ Complete tag reference

docs.title: Docker
docs.text:
bash docker run -it ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:1.0.0

→ Kubernetes Usage | Docker Usage

docs.title: The Problem
docs.text:
Modern production containers often ship without basic debugging tools. When something breaks:

```bash kubectl exec -it my-pod -- curl

docs.title: curl: not found
docs.text:
```

Distroless, scratch, and minimal Alpine images are great for production — but they leave you blind when debugging.

docs.title: Existing Solutions
docs.text:
• netshoot (201.67 MB): Great tools, but overkill for a simple curl test
  • busybox (1.5 MB): Tiny, but lacks real debugging tools
  • Alpine (7.6 MB): Minimal but not debugging-focused

docs.title: The DebugBox Answer
docs.text:
Three purpose-built variants so you pull only what you need:

Variant Size When to Use lite ~14 MB Quick network/DNS checks balanced ~46 MB Daily Kubernetes troubleshooting (default) power ~104 MB Deep forensics, packet capture

Result: Up to 14× smaller than netshoot for basic tasks. 4.3× faster pulls for common cases.

docs.title: Comparison
docs.text: Feature DebugBox netshoot busybox Multiple size options ✓ (3 variants) ✗ ✗ Smallest option 14 MB 201 MB 1.5 MB Kubernetes-focused ✓ (kubectx/kubens) ✓ ✗ Multi-arch support ✓ (amd64+arm64) ✓ ✓ Pinned tool versions ✓ ✗ ✗ Security scanned (Trivy) ✓ ✗ ✗

docs.title: Core Principles
docs.text:
• Variants over bloat — No unnecessary tools cluttering your images
  • Speed — Faster incident response with minimal pull times
  • Transparency — Every tool documented in the manifest
  • Debug-first — Runs as root for full debugging access (ephemeral use only)

docs.title: Bandwidth Savings
docs.text:
Scenario: 50 pulls per week across your cluster

Container Per Pull 50 Pulls Monthly netshoot 201.67 MB 10.08 GB 40 GB DebugBox lite 14.36 MB 718 MB 2.8 GB Savings 187.31 MB 9.36 GB 37.2 GB

On bandwidth-constrained networks, this is hours saved per month.

docs.title: Speed Example
docs.text:
netshoot @ 100 Mbps: 0m16s (201.67 MB) DebugBox lite @ 100 Mbps: 0m1s (14.36 MB)

→ Start with Quick Start or explore variants

docs.title: Debug a Running Pod (Most Common)
docs.text:
bash kubectl debug my-pod -it --image=ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox

Attaches an ephemeral debugging container with the same network/process namespace as your pod.

docs.title: Choose the Right Variant
docs.text:
Need Variant Image Quick DNS/connectivity lite ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:lite General troubleshooting (recommended) balanced ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox Packet capture & forensics power ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:power

When in doubt, use balanced — it's the default.

docs.text:
Once inside the container, you have access to comprehensive debugging tools:

docs.title: Bandwidth Savings Analysis
docs.text: How DebugBox reduces bandwidth costs and speeds up debugging

docs.title: Quick Impact
docs.text:
Scenario: 50 debug pulls per week across your cluster

Container Per Pull 50 Pulls/week Monthly netshoot v0.15 ~202 MB ~10.1 GB ~40 GB DebugBox balanced ~46 MB ~2.3 GB ~9.2 GB DebugBox lite ~14 MB ~0.7 GB ~2.8 GB Savings (lite vs netshoot) ~188 MB ~9.4 GB ~37 GB

Pull speed (100 Mbps, real-world with overhead):

  • netshoot: ~20–30 seconds
  • DebugBox lite: ~1.5–2.5 seconds
  • Difference: ~18–28 seconds per pull × 50 = 15–23 minutes saved per week

Real-world times include TLS handshake, registry latency, layer verification, and decompression.

docs.title: Real-World Scenarios
docs.text: All scenarios reference the Quick Impact table above. Use it as your baseline.

docs.title: Scenario 1: Edge Cluster (Limited Bandwidth)
docs.text:
Environment: 10 Mbps shared internet at remote facility, 20 pods/week to debug

With DebugBox lite:

  • Per pull: ~20–30 seconds (vs ~160–250 seconds netshoot)¹
  • 20 pulls/week: ~7–10 minutes total (vs ~53–83 minutes netshoot)
  • Weekly bandwidth: ~287 MB (vs ~4 GB netshoot)
  • Monthly savings: ~3+ hours + ~15 GB

Real-world speeds vary by network conditions, latency, and registry response time.

docs.title: Scenario 2: IoT/Raspberry Pi Cluster
docs.text:
Environment: 25 Pi nodes, 50 Mbps shared wireless, 75 pulls/week

With DebugBox lite:

  • Per pull: ~3–6 seconds (vs ~32–50 seconds netshoot)
  • 75 pulls/week: ~4–7 minutes total (vs ~40–63 minutes netshoot)
  • Memory footprint: ~15 MB per pull (vs ~80+ MB netshoot)
  • Alpine base boot time: < 1 second

Key benefit: Minimal resource overhead + fast iteration on tiny nodes

docs.title: Scenario 3: Mobile Networks (LTE/5G)
docs.text:
Environment: 4G LTE, 10–50 Mbps variable, metered/expensive data

Speed comparison at 20 Mbps (real-world):

  • netshoot: ~40–60 seconds
  • DebugBox lite: ~3–6 seconds

Practical impact:

  • Faster pull = less timeout risk (especially on flaky connections)
  • Smaller file = cheaper failed retries
  • Lower monthly data bills
  • Faster incident response

docs.title: Scenario 4: CI/CD Pipeline (100+ pulls/day)
docs.text:
Setup: Test framework pulls debug container per test that needs debugging

Current (netshoot):

  • 100 tests × 201 MB = ~20 GB/day
  • 20 GB × 30 days = ~600 GB/month

With DebugBox:

  • Baseline (lite): 100 × 14 MB = 1.4 GB/day = ~42 GB/month
  • Advanced (balanced): 10 × 46 MB = 0.46 GB/day = ~14 GB/month
  • Total: ~56 GB/month
  • Savings: ~544 GB/month (91% reduction)

See Cost Analysis for financial impact.

docs.title: Bandwidth by Use Case
docs.text:
Use Case Best Variant Size Reasoning DNS/API checks Lite ~14 MB Minimal: curl, dig, netcat Daily K8s troubleshooting Balanced ~46 MB Full: tcpdump, strace, vim, git SRE forensics Power ~104 MB Complete: tshark, iptables, bird

Key insight: Pick the smallest variant that solves your problem.

docs.title: Pull Speed by Bandwidth (Real-World with Overhead)
docs.text:
Speed Netshoot Lite Balanced Power 100 Mbps ~20–30s ~1.5–2.5s ~4–7s ~8–15s 50 Mbps ~40–60s ~3–6s ~8–14s ~16–30s 10 Mbps ~200–300s ~15–30s ~40–80s ~100–180s

Note: Real-world times include TLS, registry latency, decompression, and layer verification. Actual performance depends on network conditions and registry responsiveness.

docs.title: AWS NAT Gateway Egress
docs.text:
Rate: $0.045/GB ($32/TB)

Scenario: 50 pulls/week

Container Per week Monthly Annual netshoot ~10 GB $0.45 $5.40 DebugBox ~2.3 GB $0.10 $1.20 Savings $0.35 $18.20 $218

docs.title: Metered Mobile Data (High-Cost Scenario)
docs.text:
Rate: $7.50/GB (typical high-cost carrier)

Scenario: 50 pulls/week

Container Per week Monthly Annual netshoot ~$75 ~$323 ~$3,876 DebugBox ~$17 ~$74 ~$889 Savings $58 $249 $2,987

Scale to 10 engineers: ~$29,870/year saved (high-cost metered scenarios only)

Note: Costs vary significantly by carrier, region, and data plan. Average commercial rates are typically $1–3/GB.

docs.title: 2. Pre-Pull During Off-Peak
docs.text: Pull during low-traffic windows; let container runtime cache.

docs.title: 3. Set Up Registry Mirror (For Teams with High Volume)
docs.text: bash docker pull ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:lite docker tag ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:lite private-registry/debugbox:lite docker push private-registry/debugbox:lite

docs.title: 4. Use ImagePullPolicy
docs.text: yaml spec: ephemeralContainers: - image: ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:lite imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent # Don't re-pull if cached

docs.title: Recommendations by Network
docs.text: Bandwidth Lite Balanced Power Strategy > 100 Mbps ✅ ✅ ✅ Any variant works; balanced is daily driver 10–100 Mbps ✅✅ ✅ — Default to lite; use balanced on-demand < 10 Mbps ✅✅✅ — — Always lite; pre-pull during off-peak Metered ✅✅✅ — — Lite only; cache locally when possible

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
DebugBox's variant approach saves:

  • 📊 ~37 GB/month on 50 pulls vs netshoot
  • ⏱️ ~15–23 minutes/week on 100 Mbps
  • 💰 $200–$3,000/year in costs (depending on scenario)
  • 📈 Scales exponentially with team size and pull frequency

Choose right size. Save bandwidth. Debug faster. 🚀

Next step: See Image Tags Guide for production pinning strategy.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: Real-world debugging scenarios covering every tool in DebugBox.

docs.title: HTTP endpoint testing
docs.text: curl -v http://my-service:8080/health curl -I https://example.com # Headers only wget -O- http://my-service/status # Alternative HTTP client

docs.title: Port testing
docs.text: nc -zv my-service 8080 # Check if port is open nc -l 9000 # Listen on port (in one terminal) nc my-service 9000 # Connect (in another terminal)

docs.title: Routing table
docs.text: ip route ip route get 8.8.8.8 # Show route to specific IP

docs.title: Active connections
docs.text: ss -tunap # All TCP/UDP connections with processes ss -tulnp # Listening sockets only ss -t state established # Established TCP connections

docs.title: What's using a specific port?
docs.text: lsof -i :8080 lsof -i TCP:80 lsof -p $(pgrep nginx) # Files opened by nginx ```

docs.title: Real-time route tracing with statistics
docs.text: mtr example.com mtr -c 10 -r example.com # 10 cycles, report mode

docs.title: Real-time bandwidth per connection
docs.text: iftop -i eth0 iftop -i eth0 -n # Don't resolve hostnames iftop -i eth0 -P # Show port numbers ```

docs.title: Capture packets
docs.text: tcpdump -i eth0 -w /tmp/capture.pcap port 443 tcpdump -i eth0 -n 'tcp port 80' # Filter by port tcpdump -i eth0 'host 10.0.0.1' # Filter by host ```

docs.title: Network grep
docs.text: ngrep -d any "GET" tcp port 80 ngrep "mystring" tcp port 443 ```

docs.title: Parallel ping multiple hosts
docs.text: fping -a 10.0.0.1 10.0.0.2 10.0.0.3 fping -g 10.0.0.0/24 # Entire subnet fping -u 10.0.0.0/24 # Unreachable hosts only

docs.title: Trace system calls
docs.text: strace -p $(pgrep nginx) strace -e trace=network -p 1234 # Network calls only strace -e trace=file -p 1234 # File operations only strace -c curl http://example.com # Call summary

docs.title: Power variant: Library call tracing
docs.text: kubectl exec -it debug-power -- bash ltrace -p $(pgrep nginx) ltrace -c curl http://example.com # Library call summary

docs.title: Open files
docs.text: lsof -p 1234 lsof /var/log/app.log # What process has this file open? ```

docs.title: nftables (modern alternative)
docs.text: nft list ruleset nft list table inet filter

docs.title: Connection tracking
docs.text:
conntrack -L conntrack -L -p tcp --dport 443 conntrack -E # Event monitoring ```

Docker: ```bash docker run --rm -it --cap-add=NET_ADMIN ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:power

docs.title: Bridge Networking
docs.text:
Tools: brctl (bridge-utils) (power + NET_ADMIN)

```bash kubectl apply -f \ https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ibtisam-iq/debugbox/main/examples/power-debug-pod.yaml

kubectl exec -it debug-power -- bash

docs.title: Note: BIRD is a routing daemon, typically not running in debug container
Useful for testing BGP configurations if bird daemon is started separately
If bird daemon running
docs.text: birdc show status birdc show route birdc show protocols ```

docs.title: JSON processing
docs.text: curl -s https://api.github.com/users/octocat | jq '.name' kubectl get pods -o json | jq '.items[].metadata.name' echo '{"name":"test"}' | jq '.name'

docs.title: YAML processing
docs.text: kubectl get pod my-pod -o yaml | yq '.spec.containers[].image' cat config.yaml | yq '.database.host' ```

docs.title: Archive operations
docs.text: tar -czf backup.tar.gz /app/logs/ tar -xzf backup.tar.gz tar -tzf backup.tar.gz # List contents gzip large-file.log gunzip large-file.log.gz

docs.title: Edit files
docs.text: vim /etc/config.yaml nano /etc/config.yaml # Power variant, easier for beginners

docs.title: Page through output
docs.text: ps aux | less kubectl logs my-pod | less ```

docs.title: Helper Functions (Shell)
docs.text:
Built-in helpers (balanced & power variants)

```bash kubectl run helper-demo --rm -it --image=ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox --restart=Never

docs.title: Pretty-print YAML
docs.text:
kubectl get pod my-pod -o yaml | yaml ```

Power-only helpers (require NET_ADMIN): ```bash kubectl exec -it debug-power -- bash

docs.title: ConfigMap/Secret Inspection
docs.text:
Combining local kubectl with DebugBox tools:

DebugBox doesn't include kubectl, but you can pipe from your local machine:

```bash

docs.title: Process ConfigMap with jq
docs.text: kubectl get cm my-config -o json | kubectl run jq-tool --rm -i --image=ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:lite --restart=Never -- jq '.data'

docs.title: Decode and inspect secret certificate
docs.text: kubectl get secret my-tls -o jsonpath='{.data.tls.crt}' | base64 -d | \ kubectl run cert-tool --rm -i --image=ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:power --restart=Never -- openssl x509 -text -noout ```

docs.title: Variant: Lite (~14 MB)
docs.text:
• DNS: dig, nslookup, host
  • HTTP: curl, wget
  • Connectivity: ping, nc
  • Data: jq, yq
  • Basic IP: ip, ss (limited)

docs.title: Variant: Balanced (~46 MB) — Default
docs.text:
• All Lite tools, plus:
  • Advanced networking: socat, mtr, nmap
  • Monitoring: iftop, htop
  • Packet capture: tcpdump
  • Process tools: ps, top, pstree, killall, fuser
  • Tracing: strace, lsof
  • Bandwidth: iperf3
  • NIC: ethtool
  • Files: file, tar, gzip, vim
  • Version control: git

docs.title: Variant: Power (~104 MB) — Full Forensics
docs.text:
• All Balanced tools, plus:
  • Deep packet analysis: tshark, ngrep
  • Advanced scanning: nping, NSE scripts
  • TLS/SSL: openssl
  • Routing: tcptraceroute, fping
  • Library tracing: ltrace
  • Firewall: iptables, nftables (need NET_ADMIN)
  • Connection tracking: conntrack (need NET_ADMIN)
  • Bridge tools: brctl (need NET_ADMIN)
  • BGP: bird
  • Bandwidth: speedtest-cli
  • Editor: nano
  • Scripting: Python 3 + pip3

docs.title: Capability Requirements Reference
docs.text:
Tool Capability Docker Kubernetes Most tools None Standard run kubectl run/debug tshark NET_ADMIN --cap-add=NET_ADMIN Use manifest ngrep NET_ADMIN --cap-add=NET_ADMIN Use manifest iptables NET_ADMIN --cap-add=NET_ADMIN Use manifest nftables NET_ADMIN --cap-add=NET_ADMIN Use manifest conntrack NET_ADMIN --cap-add=NET_ADMIN Use manifest brctl NET_ADMIN --cap-add=NET_ADMIN Use manifest

→ Kubernetes Usage | Docker Usage | Troubleshooting | Variants Overview

docs.title: Correct formats
docs.text:
ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox # balanced (default) ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:lite # lightweight ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:power # full forensics ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:1.0.0 # production (pinned) ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:lite-1.0.0 # lite pinned version ```

Step 2: Check network connectivity ```bash

docs.title: From your node, can you reach GHCR?
If timeout/blocked, GHCR is unreachable (firewall, proxy, DNS)
docs.text:
```

Step 3: If GHCR unreachable or auth required

  • Option A: Configure image pull secret for ghcr.io ```bash kubectl create secret docker-registry ghcr-secret \ --docker-server=ghcr.io \ --docker-username= \ --docker-password=

    kubectl patch serviceaccount default -p '{"imagePullSecrets":[{"name":"ghcr-secret"}]}' ```

    • Option B: Use Docker Hub mirror (if available) bash --image=docker.io/mibtisam/debugbox

    • Option C: Pre-pull to nodes (if you have node access) bash # On each node docker pull ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:lite

    Step 4: Check pod events for details ```bash kubectl describe pod debug-pod

docs.title: Look for "Events" section with specific error
Tool Not Found / Command Not Found
docs.text:
Symptoms: bash: command not found, vim: not found, tcpdump: not found

Cause: Using wrong variant. Each variant has different tools.

Solution: Pick right variant

Tool Missing Variant Image Why bash, sh, ash lite+ Need balanced+ Lite has only ash (minimal shell) vim, git, curl lite Need balanced+ Lite is ~14MB minimal tcpdump, strace, lsof lite Need balanced+ Lite doesn't have advanced tools tshark, nftables, ltrace balanced Need power Power adds forensics tools bird, brctl, speedtest balanced Need power Power adds advanced networking iptables, conntrack balanced* Need power *Available in balanced but needs NET_ADMIN capability

Tool availability by variant:

Variant Size Includes lite ~14 MB curl, wget, dig, nslookup, jq, yq, ash, vi balanced ~46 MB lite + bash, git, vim, tcpdump, strace, lsof, ps, top, htop, nc, socat, mtr, nmap, iperf3, and 10+ more power ~104 MB balanced + tshark, ngrep, ltrace, nping, nmap-scripts, openssl, iptables, nftables, conntrack, bird, brctl, python3, nano, speedtest-cli, and more

Quick fix: ```bash

docs.title: If tool not found, try balanced
If still not found, try power
Permission Denied (tcpdump, iptables, conntrack, tshark)
docs.text:
Symptoms: tcpdump: permission denied iptables: permission denied conntrack v1.4.8: Operation failed: sorry, you must be root or get CAP_NET_ADMIN capability tshark: Couldn't run dumpcap in child process: Operation not permitted

Root cause: Missing Linux capabilities (NET_ADMIN, NET_RAW)

Quick diagnosis: ```bash

docs.title: Which capability do you need?
docs.text: tshark, ngrep, iptables, nftables, conntrack, brctl → NET_ADMIN

docs.title: Check current capabilities
docs.text:
grep Cap /proc/self/status ```

Solutions (in priority order):

Option 1: Use manifest with capabilities (Recommended) ```bash kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ibtisam-iq/debugbox/main/examples/power-debug-pod.yaml kubectl exec -it debug-power -- bash

docs.title: Now these work:
docs.text:
tshark -i eth0 iptables -L -nv conntrack -L ```

Option 2: Add capabilities to kubectl run ```bash

docs.title: For firewall tools
docs.text:
kubectl run debug --rm -it \ --image=ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:power \ --overrides='{"spec":{"containers":[{"name":"debug","securityContext":{"capabilities":{"add":["NET_ADMIN"]}}}]}}' \ --restart=Never ```

Option 3: Docker equivalent bash docker run --rm -it --cap-add=NET_RAW --cap-add=NET_ADMIN ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:power

docs.title: Container Exits Immediately
docs.text:
Symptoms: Pod runs for 1 second and exits. Status: Completed but you wanted interactive shell.

Cause 1: Missing -it flags ```bash

docs.title: kubectl debug Not Working
docs.text:
Symptoms: kubectl debug: command not found or error: debug is not a kubectl command

Cause: kubectl debug is only available in Kubernetes 1.23+

Check version: ```bash kubectl version --short

docs.title: Use:
docs.text:
kubectl run debug --rm -it --image=ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox --restart=Never ```

If kubectl is old: Update kubectl locally ```bash

docs.title: DNS Not Working Inside Container
docs.text:
Symptoms: bash dig example.com → timeout/SERVFAIL nslookup kubernetes.default → no answer curl http://my-service.default.svc.cluster.local → Connection refused

Step 1: Check resolv.conf ```bash cat /etc/resolv.conf

docs.title: Should show cluster DNS server (usually 10.96.0.10 for minikube)
docs.text:
```

Step 2: Test specific DNS server bash dig @10.96.0.10 kubernetes.default dig @8.8.8.8 google.com # If you can reach internet

Step 3: If DNS broken, check cluster ```bash

docs.title: From your local machine:
docs.text: kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep coredns kubectl logs -n kube-system

docs.title: Check if CoreDNS service exists
docs.text:
kubectl get svc -n kube-system kube-dns ```

Step 4: Quick workaround ```bash

docs.title: Add manual DNS entry inside container
Or use IP directly if you have it
Slow Image Pull
docs.text:
Symptoms: Pod takes 30+ seconds to start, image pull takes most of time

Option 1: Use lite (fastest) bash --image=ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:lite # 14 MB (~1-2 seconds)

Option 2: Pre-pull image to nodes (for frequent use) ```bash

docs.title: Or via DaemonSet (automatic on all nodes)
docs.text: kubectl apply -f - <<EOF apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: DaemonSet metadata: name: debugbox-prepull namespace: kube-system spec: selector: matchLabels: app: debugbox-prepull template: metadata: labels: app: debugbox-prepull spec: containers: - name: prepull image: ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:lite command: ["sleep", "999999"] terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 0 EOF

docs.title: Clean up when done
docs.text:
kubectl delete daemonset debugbox-prepull -n kube-system ```

Option 3: Check network ```bash

docs.title: From your node, test GHCR connection
Architecture Mismatch (ARM64 nodes)
docs.text:
Symptoms: exec format error, pod fails to run on ARM nodes

Status: NOT AN ISSUE - DebugBox images are multi-arch

Verification: ```bash

docs.title: - arm64 (Apple Silicon, AWS Graviton, Raspberry Pi)
Images include both architectures
docs.text:
docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64 -t debugbox . ```

If you still get exec format error: ```bash

docs.title: Check if you're actually on the right architecture
If mismatch, the image might be corrupted - try re-pulling
"No such file or directory" on vim/nano commands
docs.text:
Symptoms: vim: command not found (when you use lite) but you swear you saw it before

Cause: Using lite variant. Lite only has vi, not full vim

Solution: ```bash

docs.title: Frequently Asked Questions
docs.text:
Q: Does DebugBox include kubectl? A: No — intentionally. DebugBox is for low-level debugging inside containers. Use kubectl locally and pipe output to DebugBox if needed.

Q: Can I run as non-root? A: Yes. Most tools work fine as non-root. Only tools needing raw sockets (tshark) or system access (strace, iptables) require root/capabilities.

Q: Why no helm, k9s, or kustomize? A: DebugBox focuses on debugging inside containers (network, processes, files). Deployment/management tools are out of scope.

Q: Does DebugBox work with Kubernetes 1.18? A: Yes for most things. kubectl debug requires 1.23+. Use kubectl run for older clusters.

Q: Can I use DebugBox outside Kubernetes? A: Absolutely. It's just a Docker container: bash docker run -it ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox docker run -it ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:lite docker run -it ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:power

Q: When should I use each variant?

  • Lite: You only need DNS/HTTP checks (saves bandwidth)
  • Balanced: Default for most debugging (best balance)
  • Power: You need packet analysis, firewall debugging, or Python scripting

Q: What's included in lite? A: curl, wget, dig, nslookup, host, jq, yq, ash, vi, less

Q: How do I pin versions in production? A: Always use specific version tags: bash ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:1.0.0 ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:lite-1.0.0 ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:power-1.0.0 Never use :latest in production manifests.

Q: Can I extend DebugBox with my own tools? A: Yes, create a custom image: dockerfile FROM ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:1.0.0 RUN apk add --no-cache my-package-name

Q: How often are new versions released? A: As needed. Track releases: ```bash

docs.title: Or visit
docs.text:
https://github.com/ibtisam-iq/debugbox/releases ```

Q: Does DebugBox work on Docker Desktop? A: Yes. docker run -it ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox works on Mac/Windows/Linux.

Q: What if I need to debug a pod that's not running? A: You can't attach to a crashed pod. Options: ```bash

docs.title: 2. Create a new pod with same image
3. Use a sidecar pattern for permanent debugging
docs.text:
```

Q: How do I share debug sessions with colleagues? A: Use socat for port forwarding: ```bash

docs.title: Still Stuck?
docs.text:
1. Check the Examples → Examples has 40+ real scenarios

2. Check your cluster → Is CoreDNS running? Node ready? bash kubectl get nodes kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep coredns

3. Verify image access → Can you pull locally? bash docker pull ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:lite

4. Open GitHub issue with:

  • DebugBox variant/version used: ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:power-1.0.0
  • Kubernetes version: kubectl version --short
  • Exact command: kubectl debug my-pod -it --image=...
  • Error message (full output, not summarized)
  • Your cluster setup (minikube, EKS, GKE, etc.)

→ Examples | Kubernetes Usage | Docker Usage | GitHub Issues

docs.title: Tooling Manifest
docs.text:
The authoritative list of tools, shell behavior, and environment guarantees in each DebugBox variant.

This page is derived from the source-of-truth manifest.yaml in the repository.

All variants run as root by design (required for debugging privileges).

docs.title: Variant Inheritance
docs.text:
• lite → builds on base
  • balanced → builds on lite
  • power → builds on balanced

Tools marked with ✓ are included in that variant and all higher tiers.

docs.title: Shell & Environment
docs.text: Feature Base Lite Balanced Power Default shell ash ash bash bash Prompt ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ Editor (default) vi vi vim vim Additional editor — — — nano Pager — — less less Locale C.UTF-8 C.UTF-8 C.UTF-8 C.UTF-8

docs.title: Shell Helpers (/etc/profile.d)
docs.text: Helper Lite Balanced Power Description ll() ✓ ✓ ✓ ls -alF alias json() ✓ ✓ ✓ Pretty-print JSON yaml() ✓ ✓ ✓ Pretty-print YAML ports() — ✓ ✓ List listening ports connections() — ✓ ✓ Show active connections routes() — ✓ ✓ Display routing table k8s-info — ✓ ✓ K8s context & namespace sniff() — — ✓ Quick packet capture sniff-http() — — ✓ Capture HTTP (80/443) sniff-dns() — — ✓ Capture DNS queries cert-check() — — ✓ Inspect TLS certs conntrack-watch() — — ✓ Monitor connections

docs.title: Networking
docs.text: Tool Lite Balanced Power curl ✓ ✓ ✓ netcat-openbsd ✓ ✓ ✓ iproute2 (ip) ✓ ✓ ✓ iputils (ping) ✓ ✓ ✓ bind-tools (dig, nslookup) ✓ ✓ ✓ tcpdump — ✓ ✓ socat — ✓ ✓ nmap — ✓ ✓ mtr — ✓ ✓ iperf3 — ✓ ✓ ethtool — ✓ ✓ iftop — ✓ ✓ tshark — — ✓ ngrep — — ✓ tcptraceroute — — ✓ fping — — ✓ speedtest-cli — — ✓ nmap-nping — — ✓ nmap-scripts (NSE) — — ✓

docs.title: System & Process
docs.text: Tool Lite Balanced Power htop — ✓ ✓ strace — ✓ ✓ lsof — ✓ ✓ procps — ✓ ✓ psmisc — ✓ ✓ ltrace — — ✓

docs.title: Kubernetes & Control Plane
docs.text: Tool Lite Balanced Power kubectx — ✓ ✓ kubens — ✓ ✓

docs.title: Data Processing & Scripting
docs.text: Tool Lite Balanced Power jq ✓ ✓ ✓ yq ✓ ✓ ✓ python3 + pip3 — — ✓

docs.title: Filesystem & Version Control
docs.text: Tool Lite Balanced Power file — ✓ ✓ tar, gzip — ✓ ✓ git — ✓ ✓

docs.title: Routing & Firewall (Power Only)
docs.text: Tool Power iptables ✓ nftables ✓ conntrack-tools ✓ bird ✓ bridge-utils ✓

docs.title: Guarantees
docs.text:
• Deterministic: Critical tools (e.g., yq in power) are version-pinned and SHA-verified
  • Transparent: Only documented tools included — no hidden packages
  • Secure by default: Scanned with Trivy on every release
  • Root access: All images run as root for full debugging capability (ephemeral use only)

→ Variants Overview | Image Tags | Examples

docs.title: Multi-Architecture Support
docs.text: All DebugBox images are multi-platform manifests supporting both common CPU architectures.

docs.title: Supported Architectures
docs.text:
• linux/amd64 (x86_64) — Intel/AMD servers, most cloud VMs, GitHub Actions
  • linux/arm64 (aarch64) — Apple Silicon (M1–M5), AWS Graviton, Raspberry Pi 64-bit

docs.title: Automatic Selection
docs.text:
Docker, containerd, and Kubernetes automatically pull the correct architecture for your platform — no manual configuration needed.

```bash

docs.title: Works seamlessly on both amd64 and arm64
docs.text:
docker pull ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:1.0.0 kubectl debug my-pod -it --image=ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:1.0.0 ```

The registry returns the correct image for your CPU architecture automatically.

docs.title: Explicit Platform (Rare)
docs.text:
To explicitly request a specific architecture (for testing or compatibility):

```bash

docs.title: Force arm64 on an amd64 machine
Multi-Architecture Build Process
docs.text:
Every DebugBox release builds all three variants for both architectures:

Variant amd64 arm64 lite ✓ ✓ balanced ✓ ✓ power ✓ ✓

This ensures feature parity and performance across all platforms.

→ Variants Overview | Tooling Manifest →

docs.title: Image Tags & Registries
docs.text: DebugBox is published to two identical registries with 20 tags per release using a single-repository variant strategy.

docs.title: Registries
docs.text: Registry URL Primary? GitHub Container Registry (GHCR) ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox Yes (recommended) Docker Hub docker.io/mibtisam/debugbox Mirror

docs.title: Tag Strategy
docs.text: All three variants in one repository with variant-based tagging:

docs.title: Primary Tags (Variant Discovery)
docs.text: bash ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:lite # Latest lite ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:balanced # Latest balanced ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:power # Latest power

docs.title: Default Aliases (Convenience, Balanced Only)
docs.text: bash ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:latest # Alias to balanced-latest ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:1.0.0 # Alias to balanced-1.0.0

docs.title: Quick Reference
docs.text: Use Case Example Development (latest balanced) ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox Latest lite ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:lite Latest power ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:power Production (balanced) ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:1.0.0 Production (lite) ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:lite-1.0.0 Production (power) ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:power-1.0.0

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
Development:

  • Use :latest or variant name for interactive debugging
  • Example: docker run -it ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:lite

Production & CI/CD:

  • Always pin specific version and variant
  • Never use :latest in production manifests
  • Example: ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:balanced-1.0.0

docs.title: Versioning
docs.text:
DebugBox follows Semantic Versioning (MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH):

  • v1.0.0 — First stable release
  • v1.0.1 — Patch (bug fix)
  • v1.1.0 — Minor (features, backward compatible)
  • v2.0.0 — Major (breaking changes)

Key Rules:

  • Released versions are immutable
  • Older versions remain available forever
  • :latest always points to newest stable (never pre-releases)

docs.title: Total Tags Per Release
docs.text:
Category Pattern Count Primary discovery :lite, :balanced, :power 3 Floating version :-latest (3 variants) 3 Pinned version :-1.0.0 (3 variants) 3 Default aliases :latest, :1.0.0 2 Per registry — 11 tags Both registries GHCR + Docker Hub 20 tags

→ Installation | Variants Overview | Quick Start

docs.title: Security Policy
docs.text: DebugBox takes security seriously. All released images follow strict practices to minimize risk.

docs.title: Vulnerability Reporting
docs.text:
Do not open public GitHub issues for security vulnerabilities.

Please report privately via Email.

Subject: [SECURITY] DebugBox vulnerability: [brief description]

Include:

  • Description and steps to reproduce
  • Affected variant and version (e.g., power-1.0.0)
  • Potential impact and severity assessment

We follow responsible disclosure:

  • Acknowledgment: Within 72 hours
  • Investigation: Details shared via private discussion
  • Fix timeline: Based on severity (HIGH/CRITICAL: immediate)
  • Patch release: Before public announcement
  • Credit: Given if desired

→ Full policy on GitHub: SECURITY.md

docs.title: Automated Vulnerability Scanning
docs.text:
Every release is scanned with Trivy:

  • HIGH and CRITICAL vulnerabilities block release entirely
  • All three variants (lite, balanced, power) scanned
  • Both linux/amd64 and linux/arm64 validated
  • Results logged in GitHub Actions workflow

Why only amd64 scanning before push?

  • 99% of vulnerabilities are in shared base layers (OS packages, dependencies)
  • amd64 and arm64 have identical software stacks (only CPU instruction sets differ)
  • Industry standard: Docker Official Images, Python, Node, Redis all follow this pattern

Local scanning: bash make scan

docs.title: Image Design Security
docs.text:
• Runs as root — required for tools like tcpdump, strace (ephemeral debugging only)
  • No baked-in secrets — credentials not compiled in
  • Minimal variants — lite excludes compilers/development tools to reduce attack surface
  • Pinned dependencies — critical tools version-locked and SHA-verified where possible
  • Alpine Linux base — minimal (~3.9 MB) attack surface vs. Debian/Ubuntu

docs.title: Release Security
docs.text:
From RELEASE.md:

Version Support Duration Security Patches Current (v1.x) 12 months All severities Previous (v1.x-1) 6 months HIGH/CRITICAL only Older (v1.x-2+) Community/best-effort None guaranteed

Example: When v1.1.0 is released:

  • v1.0.x gets 6 months of security-only patches
  • v0.9.x shifts to community support

docs.title: Immutable Released Images
docs.text:
• Released versions never change — once published, images are immutable
  • Older versions remain available forever — for historical reference or rollback
  • If a vulnerability is found:
    1. Patched version released immediately (e.g., v1.0.0 → v1.0.1)
    2. Old release marked with warning in GitHub Releases
    3. Users on :latest auto-receive fix
    4. Users on pinned versions can upgrade at their discretion

docs.title: Always pin version AND variant
docs.text:
kubectl debug my-pod -it --image=ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:1.0.0 docker run -it ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:lite-1.0.0 ```

Never use :latest in production manifests.

docs.title: Variant Selection
docs.text:
• Use lite when possible (14 MB, smallest attack surface)
  • Upgrade to balanced for standard debugging (46 MB)
  • Use power only when needed for advanced forensics (104 MB)

docs.title: Runtime Hygiene
docs.text:
• Run in isolated namespaces
  • Delete debugging pods immediately after use
  • Don't leave debugging containers running in production
  • Monitor GitHub security advisories: watch → Custom → Security alerts

docs.title: Multi-Architecture Considerations
docs.text:
• DebugBox is identical across amd64 and arm64 (same security guarantees)
  • No architecture-specific vulnerabilities known
  • Both architectures scanned before release

docs.title: Known Vulnerabilities & Suppressions
docs.text:
DebugBox is transparent about any CVEs in optional dependencies:

Check SECURITY.md for:

  • List of suppressed vulnerabilities (if any)
  • Rationale for each suppression
  • Risk assessment per variant

docs.title: Acknowledgments
docs.text:
Thank you to security researchers who report issues responsibly.

Hall of Fame: No reports yet — help us keep DebugBox secure!

docs.title: Quick Links
docs.text:
• Report vulnerability: Email
  • GitHub Security Advisories: github.com/ibtisam-iq/debugbox/security
  • Releases & Security Notes: github.com/ibtisam-iq/debugbox/releases
  • Subscribe to alerts: Watch repository → Custom → Security alerts

docs.title: Docker Usage
docs.text: DebugBox integrates seamlessly with Docker for local development and container inspection.

docs.title: Power Variant with Capabilities
docs.text:
⚠️ For advanced networking tools (tshark, conntrack, nft, iptables), add Linux capabilities:

bash docker run --rm -it \ --cap-add=NET_ADMIN \ --cap-add=NET_RAW \ ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:power

docs.title: Share Another Container's Network
docs.text:
Inspect another container's network namespace:

bash docker run -it --rm \ --net container:my-app \ ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox

docs.title: Host Network Access
docs.text:
Debug the Docker host directly:

bash docker run -it --rm --net host ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox

docs.title: Volume Mount for Artifacts
docs.text:
Save packet captures or logs to host:

```bash docker run -it --rm \ -v $(pwd)/captures:/captures \ --cap-add=NET_ADMIN \ ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:power

docs.title: Docker Compose Sidecar
docs.text: Add a debugging sidecar to your compose file:

docs.title: Standard Debugging
docs.text:
```yaml services: app: image: nginx:alpine ports: ["80:80"]

debugbox: image: ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:balanced network_mode: service:app command: sleep infinity tty: true ```

docs.title: With Networking Capabilities (Power Variant)
docs.text:
```yaml services: app: image: nginx:alpine ports: ["80:80"]

debugbox-power: image: ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:power network_mode: service:app command: sleep infinity cap_add: - NET_ADMIN - NET_RAW tty: true ```

Access: ```bash

docs.title: Variant Selection Guide
docs.text:
Task Variant Command Quick connectivity test lite docker run -it --rm ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:lite General debugging balanced docker run -it --rm ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox Packet capture power + caps docker run -it --rm --cap-add=NET_ADMIN ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:power Firewall/routing power + caps docker run -it --rm --cap-add=NET_ADMIN ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:power

→ Kubernetes Usage | Real-world Examples | Troubleshooting

docs.title: Kubernetes Usage
docs.text: DebugBox is Kubernetes-native and optimized for ephemeral debugging workflows.

docs.title: Debug a Running Pod (Most Common)
docs.text:
Attach a debugging container to an existing pod:

```bash

docs.title: Production (pinned version)
docs.text:
kubectl debug my-pod -it --image=ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:1.0.0 ```

Shares network and process namespace with the target pod.

docs.title: Standalone Debugging Pod
docs.text:
Create a temporary debugging pod:

bash kubectl run debug --rm -it \ --image=ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox \ --restart=Never

Exit with exit or Ctrl+C to delete the pod.

docs.title: Power Variant with Capabilities
docs.text: ⚠️ For advanced networking tools (tshark, conntrack, nft, iptables), use a manifest with capabilities:

docs.title: Create Manually
docs.text:
yaml apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: debug-power spec: containers: - name: debugbox image: ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:power command: ["/bin/bash"] securityContext: capabilities: add: - NET_ADMIN - NET_RAW tty: true stdin: true restartPolicy: Never

Delete when done: bash kubectl delete pod debug-power

docs.title: Kubernetes Context Switching
docs.text:
DebugBox includes kubectx and kubens (balanced and power variants only):

```bash

docs.title: Inside the pod
docs.text:
kubectx # List contexts kubectx minikube # Switch context kubens # List namespaces kubens kube-system # Switch namespace kubectx -c # Show current context ```

Note: Context/namespace switching works because DebugBox runs in the same network as your local kubeconfig.

docs.title: Node-Level Debugging
docs.text:
Debug the cluster node itself:

bash kubectl run node-debug --rm -it \ --image=ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox \ --overrides='{"spec":{"hostNetwork":true}}' \ --restart=Never

docs.title: Standard Sidecar
docs.text: yaml apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: app-with-debug spec: containers: - name: app image: nginx:alpine - name: debugbox image: ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:1.0.0 command: ["sleep", "infinity"]

docs.title: Power Sidecar with Capabilities
docs.text:
yaml apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: app-with-debug-power spec: containers: - name: app image: nginx:alpine - name: debugbox image: ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:power command: ["sleep", "infinity"] securityContext: capabilities: add: - NET_ADMIN - NET_RAW

Access: bash kubectl exec -it app-with-debug -c debugbox -- bash kubectl exec -it app-with-debug-power -c debugbox -- bash

docs.title: Variant Recommendations
docs.text: Task Variant Command Quick DNS/connectivity lite kubectl debug my-pod -it --image=ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:lite Pod debugging (default) balanced kubectl debug my-pod -it --image=ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox Packet capture power + manifest kubectl apply -f power-debug-pod.yaml Routing/firewall power + manifest kubectl apply -f power-debug-pod.yaml

docs.title: Production Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Always pin versions: bash --image=ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:1.0.0 Never use :latest in production manifests.

  2. Delete after use: bash kubectl delete pod debug-session kubectl delete pod debug-power

  3. Prefer ephemeral containers (kubectl debug) over persistent sidecars for minimal impact.

  4. Only add capabilities when needed — standard kubectl debug doesn't need them.

→ Docker Usage | Real-world Examples | Troubleshooting

docs.title: Balanced Variant (Recommended)
docs.text:
~46 MB — The sweet spot for daily debugging.

The default and recommended choice for nearly all Kubernetes troubleshooting tasks.

docs.title: When to Use Balanced
docs.text:
• Debugging running application pods
  • Daily Kubernetes workflows and incident response
  • Process tracing, network inspection, system diagnostics
  • When in doubt, start here

docs.title: Pull Tags
docs.text:
Latest: bash ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox # Most common ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:latest ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:balanced

Production (pinned version): bash ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:1.0.0 # Short form (recommended) ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:balanced-1.0.0 # Explicit form

docs.title: Docker
docs.text: bash docker run -it ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox docker run -it ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:1.0.0 # Production

docs.title: What's Included
docs.text:
Balanced includes all lite tools plus 21 additional packages for system debugging, process inspection, and Kubernetes workflows.

→ Complete balanced tool list with examples

Key additions over lite:

  • Shell & Editors: bash, vim, nano
  • Process Tools: strace, lsof, htop, ps, top
  • Network Advanced: tcpdump, socat, nmap, mtr, iperf3, iftop
  • Kubernetes Helpers: kubectx, kubens
  • Version Control: git
  • Compression: tar, gzip

docs.title: Shell Helpers
docs.text:
Balanced includes 6+ custom shell functions for rapid debugging:

→ Complete shell helper reference with examples

Quick reference:

  • ll() — ls -alF alias
  • json() / yaml() — Pretty-print data
  • ports() — List listening ports
  • connections() — Show active connections
  • routes() — Display routing table
  • k8s-info — Current Kubernetes context

docs.title: When to Switch
docs.text:
Downgrade to Lite Variant? Save 32 MB if you only need basic connectivity.

Upgrade to Power Variant? Need packet analysis, routing tools, or scripting.

→ Variants Overview | Real-world Examples | Troubleshooting

docs.title: Lite Variant
docs.text:
~14 MB — Minimal and lightning-fast.

Perfect for quick network diagnostics and bandwidth-constrained environments.

docs.title: When to Use Lite
docs.text:
• Quick DNS resolution checks
  • API connectivity verification
  • Bandwidth-constrained networks or edge clusters
  • Init containers or fast pod startup
  • When you only need basic networking tools

docs.title: Docker
docs.text:
```bash docker run -it ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:lite

docker run ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:lite curl -I https://ibtisam-iq.com ```

docs.title: What's Included
docs.text:
Lite includes 8 essential networking packages optimized for fast pulls and basic connectivity testing.

→ Complete lite tool list with examples

Key categories:

  • HTTP/HTTPS clients (curl)
  • DNS tools (dig, nslookup, host)
  • Data processors (jq, yq)
  • Basic networking (netcat, ip, ping)
  • Minimal shell (ash, vi)

docs.title: When to Upgrade
docs.text:
Need more tools? → Balanced Variant adds bash, tcpdump, vim, strace, and Kubernetes helpers (+32 MB)

Need forensics? → Power Variant adds packet analysis, routing, and Python scripting (+90 MB)

→ Variants Overview | Real-world Examples | Troubleshooting

docs.title: Variants Overview
docs.text:
DebugBox provides three purpose-built variants optimized for different use cases.

Variant Size Use When Image lite ~14 MB Quick network/DNS checks ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:lite balanced (default) ~46 MB Daily Kubernetes troubleshooting ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox power ~104 MB Deep forensics, packet capture ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:power

docs.title: Choosing Your Variant
docs.text:
• Lite: For bandwidth constraints, init containers, edge devices. Limited to basic connectivity tools.
  • Balanced: Daily driver (recommended). TCP dump, system inspection, Kubernetes helpers included.
  • Power: When you need advanced forensics — packet analysis, routing, library tracing, Python scripting.

docs.title: Detailed Pages
docs.text:
• Lite — Minimal (14 MB)
  • Balanced — Recommended (46 MB)
  • Power — Comprehensive (104 MB)

docs.title: Pulling Latest vs. Specific Version
docs.text:
Latest (for development): bash docker pull ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:lite # Latest lite docker pull ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox # Latest balanced docker pull ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:power # Latest power

Specific version (for production): bash docker pull ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:lite-1.0.0 docker pull ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:1.0.0 docker pull ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:power-1.0.0

→ Complete tag reference

docs.title: Inheritance Model
docs.text:
All variants build on each other:

  • Lite — Base networking tools
  • Balanced — Lite + system inspection + Kubernetes helpers
  • Power — Balanced + packet analysis + advanced routing + scripting

Every tool in lite is also in balanced and power.

→ Complete Tool List

docs.title: Power Variant
docs.text:
~104 MB — Full SRE-grade forensics toolkit.

Everything from balanced, plus advanced tools for deep system and network investigation.

docs.title: When to Use Power
docs.text:
• Detailed packet analysis with tshark
  • Library-level tracing (ltrace)
  • Advanced routing and firewall debugging
  • Custom Python scripting
  • Deep incident forensics
  • Not recommended for routine tasks — stick with balanced to save bandwidth

docs.title: ⚠️ Linux Capabilities Required
docs.text:
Power variant tools like tshark, conntrack, iptables, and nftables require additional Linux capabilities to function.

→ Kubernetes capability setup → Docker capability setup

docs.title: Apply pre-made manifest
docs.text:
kubectl apply -f \ https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ibtisam-iq/debugbox/main/examples/power-debug-pod.yaml

kubectl exec -it debug-power -- bash ```

Basic usage (no capabilities): ```bash kubectl debug my-pod -it --image=ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:power

kubectl run debug --rm -it --image=ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:power --restart=Never ```

docs.title: Docker
docs.text:
With capabilities: bash docker run --rm -it \ --cap-add=NET_ADMIN \ --cap-add=NET_RAW \ ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:power

Basic usage: bash docker run -it ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:power docker run -it ghcr.io/ibtisam-iq/debugbox:power-1.0.0 # Production

docs.title: What's Included
docs.text:
Power includes all balanced tools plus 14 advanced forensics packages for packet analysis, TLS inspection, routing, and scripting.

→ Complete power tool list with examples

Key additions over balanced:

  • Packet Analysis: tshark, ngrep (requires NET_RAW)
  • TLS/SSL: openssl
  • Routing & Firewall: iptables, nftables, conntrack, bird, brctl (requires NET_ADMIN)
  • Advanced Network: tcptraceroute, fping, speedtest-cli, nmap-nping, nmap-scripts
  • System Internals: ltrace
  • Scripting: Python 3 + pip3
  • Editors: nano (in addition to vim)

docs.title: Shell Helpers
docs.text:
Power includes all balanced helpers plus 5 advanced network functions:

→ Complete shell helper reference with examples

Power-exclusive helpers:

  • sniff() — Quick packet capture with smart filters
  • sniff-http() — Capture HTTP traffic (ports 80/443)
  • sniff-dns() — Capture DNS queries (port 53)
  • cert-check() — Inspect TLS certificates from servers
  • conntrack-watch() — Monitor active connections in real-time

docs.title: When to Downgrade
docs.text:
Most tasks use Balanced Variant. Downgrade to save 58 MB.

For speed, use Lite Variant. Saves 90 MB.

→ Variants Overview | Real-world Examples | Troubleshooting
